does: This paper addresses the tension between inference-time scaling methods and post-training reinforcement learning by proposing Exploration-Driven Optimization (EDO), which extends reward-biasing exploration objectives into iterative post-training. The core contribution is incorporating exploration mechanisms directly into standard RL objectives during training to maintain diverse probability distributions rather than sharply concentrating them. The authors instantiate EDO in two variants—ED-iDPO and ED-GRPO—by integrating exploration bonuses into iterative Direct Preference Optimization and Group Relative Policy Optimization respectively. Extensive experiments show that both variants improve solution diversity and reasoning performance, with particular gains when combined with test-time computation methods like self-consistency.
connects: This work builds on several converging research threads: the literature on inference-time scaling and test-time computation for LLMs that demonstrates benefits of sampling diversity, the emergence of RL-based post-training methods like DPO and GRPO that optimize model behavior through preference learning, and exploration research from reinforcement learning that uses bonus mechanisms to encourage visitation to diverse states. The paper directly responds to an identified limitation in recent post-training approaches—that RL optimization naturally compresses probability distributions in ways that conflict with diversity-seeking inference strategies. It extends prior work on reward-biasing exploration, traditionally applied in contextual bandits and single-episode RL, into the multi-step iterative training regime of modern LLM post-training.
enables: This work opens directions for tighter integration between training-time and test-time optimization of LLMs. By demonstrating that exploration-friendly distributions can be learned during post-training while maintaining strong reasoning performance, it suggests pathways for more efficient inference-time scaling that requires fewer samples to achieve equivalent performance. The approach potentially enables future research into principled methods for balancing convergence and diversity across the training-inference pipeline, adaptive exploration mechanisms that adjust diversity incentives based on task difficulty, and combinations of EDO with emerging test-time methods beyond self-consistency. More broadly, it points toward foundations for understanding and optimizing the interplay between model capacity, training objectives, and computational budget allocation at inference time.
